key_points: Fractals are shapes generated through recursive processes that produce self-similar patterns
Sierpinski Triangle is created by repeatedly removing the central triangle from subdivided triangles
Sierpinski Carpet is formed by dividing a square into nine smaller squares and removing the central square repeatedly
The Koch Snowflake begins with an equilateral triangle and adds smaller triangular bumps on each side
Projections help represent three-dimensional objects accurately on two-dimensional surfaces
examples: Step 1 of Sierpinski Triangle leaves 3 smaller triangles
Step 2 of Sierpinski Carpet results in 64 smaller squares
The Koch Snowflake increases the number of sides with each iteration
Projection example: A cube's shadow on a flat surface
formulas: Number of triangles after n steps in Sierpinski Triangle → 3^n
Number of squares after n steps in Sierpinski Carpet → 8^n
Number of sides in Koch Snowflake after n steps → 3 × 4^n
exam_tips: Practice drawing Sierpinski figures step by step
Remember that projections remove depth information
Study the relation between faces, edges and vertices in polyhedra
